questions.question: Explain why Thread.suspend(), resume(), and stop() methods are deprecated. What are the safer alternatives?
keyPoints: Deprecated due to potential deadlock and data inconsistency issues
suspend() holds lock indefinitely causing deadlock
stop() can leave objects in inconsistent state
Alternative: Use boolean flag to control thread execution
Use interrupt() for cooperative termination

questions.question: Explain the difference between wait() and sleep() methods in Java threads.
keyPoints: wait() releases the lock, sleep() retains the lock
wait() is called on object, sleep() is called on thread
wait() needs synchronized context, sleep() doesn't
wait() wakes up via notify(), sleep() wakes up after timeout
wait() defined in Object class, sleep() in Thread class

questions.question: What is thread synchronization? Explain the use of synchronized keyword with an example.
keyPoints: Thread synchronization prevents race conditions
synchronized keyword ensures mutual exclusion
Can be applied to methods or blocks
Only one thread can execute synchronized code on same object
Example: synchronized void deposit() { // critical section }

questions.question: Explain the notify() and notifyAll() methods with their differences.
keyPoints: Both methods are defined in Object class
Both used to wake up waiting threads
notify() wakes one random waiting thread
notifyAll() wakes all waiting threads
notifyAll() is safer when multiple conditions exist

questions.question: What is the difference between Runnable and Thread in Java?
keyPoints: Runnable is an interface with run() method
Thread is a class that implements Runnable
Runnable allows inheritance of other class
Thread provides additional methods like start(), sleep()
Prefer Runnable for better OOP design

questions.question: Explain the concept of daemon threads with an example.
keyPoints: Daemon threads are low-priority background threads
They provide services to user threads
JVM terminates when all user threads finish
Common examples: garbage collector, JIT compiler
Set using setDaemon(true) before start()

questions.question: What is thread starvation? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Starvation occurs when thread cannot access shared resources
Caused by greedy threads holding resources long
Solutions: Use fair locks, increase thread priority, avoid long synchronized blocks
Use thread pools with appropriate configuration
Avoid nested synchronized blocks

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating how to safely suspend, resume, and stop a thread using a flag variable. Explain each part of the code.
keyPoints: Create a class implementing Runnable
Include boolean suspendFlag and stopFlag
In run() method, check flags using while loop
Use wait() for suspension to reduce CPU usage
Provide methods: mySuspend(), myResume(), myStop()
Explain thread lifecycle and proper cleanup
Show main method creating and managing thread

questions.question: Explain the thread lifecycle in Java with a state diagram. Discuss all possible states and transitions between them.
keyPoints: Six states: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED
NEW: Thread created but not started
RUNNABLE: Ready to run or running
BLOCKED: Waiting for monitor lock
WAITING: wait(), join(), LockSupport.park()
TIMED_WAITING: sleep(), wait(timeout), join(timeout)
TERMINATED: run() completed
Explain transitions with methods causing each

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem and how wait() and notify() solve it. Write synchronized code for the same.
keyPoints: Problem: Producer produces data, Consumer consumes; buffer has limited capacity
Issues: Overflow when buffer full, underflow when empty
Solution: Use wait() and notify() for thread communication
Producer waits when buffer full, notifies after adding
Consumer waits when empty, notifies after consuming
Show synchronized produce() and consume() methods
Explain how monitor lock prevents race conditions

questions.question: What is deadlock? Explain the necessary conditions for deadlock and discuss strategies to avoid deadlock in Java.
keyPoints: Deadlock: Two or more threads waiting forever for each other
Four necessary conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
Prevention strategies: Avoid nested locks, Lock ordering, Try-lock with timeout
Use thread-safe collections instead of manual synchronization
Design to avoid circular wait - define lock acquisition order
Example code showing deadlock and fix
